Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * revision.comment: Text replacement - "小口山古墳}}" to "小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/soshiki/shougaigakushu/bunka-sekai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki_shokai/kofun_shumatsuki/2398.html Tsukaana Kofun] – Habikino City website {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukaana Kofun}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/soshiki/shougaigakushu/bunka-sekai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki_shokai/kofun_shumatsuki/2398.html Tsukaana Kofun] – Habikino City website {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukaana Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino)}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/soshiki/shougaigakushu/bunka-sekai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki_shokai/kofun_shumatsuki/2398.html Tsukaana Kofun] – Habikino City website {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukaana Kofun}} Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = [[Square tumulus]] | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/soshiki/shougaigakushu/bunka-sekai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki_shokai/kofun_shumatsuki/2398.html Tsukaana Kofun] – Habikino City website {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukaana Kofun}} It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == *
