Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * 10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō.== See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō.== See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also! == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | Label = Tsukaana Kofun | Label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * revision.comment: attempt at mass correcting bad parameters on indofox ancient site
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=now we have a 'Burial Facility' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Tsukaana Kofun (塚穴古墳) | image = [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | Latitude_deg = 34 | Latitude_min = 33 | Latitude_sec = 3.58 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 35 | Longitude_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | Size = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | map_dot_label = Tsukaana Kofun | map_dot_label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates_deg = 34 | coordinates_min = 33 | coordinates_sec = 3.58 | coordinates_deg = 135 | coordinates_min = 35 | coordinates_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | area = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | map_dot_label = Tsukaana Kofun | map_dot_label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | Group = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates_deg = 34 | coordinates_min = 33 | coordinates_sec = 3.58 | coordinates_deg = 135 | coordinates_min = 35 | coordinates_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | area = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | map_dot_label = Tsukaana Kofun | map_dot_label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-shaped stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == * Kofun mound | Alternate_name = Hanyū Tsukaana Kofun | part_of = [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] | location = [[Habikino, Osaka|Habikino City]], Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates_deg = 34 | coordinates_min = 33 | coordinates_sec = 3.58 | coordinates_deg = 135 | coordinates_min = 35 | coordinates_sec = 30.55 | ISO = JP-27 | type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | area = 54m on each side
10m in height | Burial_facility = Two-tiered {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} (Iwayayama style) | Artifacts_found = | Associated_tombs = | Construction_period = Early [[7th century]] | Buried_person = Determined by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as [[Prince Kume|Prince Kume]] | Tomb_designation = "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" by the Imperial Household Agency | Historic_sites = | Tangible_cultural_properties = | Special_notes = | map = Japan Osaka | Icon = 方墳 | map_dot_label = Tsukaana Kofun | map_dot_label_position = top }} [[File:Tsukaana Kofun (Habikino), haisho.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|[[Prince Kume]]'s Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka (Imperial burial)}}]] '''Tsukaana Kofun''' (塚穴古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} and is classified as a {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates it as the "Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka" (埴生崗上墓), the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}. == Overview == Located on a plateau at the northern edge of the Habikino Hills (elevation 64 meters) in eastern Osaka Prefecture, the Tsukaana Kofun is a large square tumulus, measuring approximately 53–54 meters on each side and 10 meters in height. It is part of a group of late-period kofun, including the {{ill|Hichinjoike-nishi Kofun|ja|ヒチンジョ池西古墳}} and {{ill|Oguchiyama Kofun|ja|小口山古墳|1=Koguchiyama Kofun}}. The mound is currently under the management of the Imperial Household Agency, which conducted a survey of the tumulus in 2008. The surrounding area has been surveyed by the {{ill|Habikino City Board of Education|ja|羽曳野市教育委員会}} since 2005. The mound consists of three tiers. Though no surface features like stone coverings remain visible, traces of tuff stone suggest such features once existed. Surrounding the mound is a wide moat and embankment, measuring 130 meters square in total, with the southern embankment structured in two levels and the northern embankment broader, at 38 meters wide and 3 meters high. The burial chamber, now buried, is a two-tiered yokoana-style stone chamber that opened to the south. Historical records from the Edo period and Meiji era describe its design as consistent with the Iwayayama style seen in {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}. == History == * Late [[Edo period]]: The monk Kakuhō documented the interior of the stone chamber. * 1801: Listed as "Tsukaana" in the {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}. * 1875: Designated as Hanyū Kōjō no Hakaka by the Ministry of Education. * 1890: Restoration of the tomb area, during which the stone chamber was sealed. * 1925: A survey of the site was conducted. * 2005–2006: Excavations carried out due to nearby construction. * 2008: Survey by the Imperial Household Agency. == Burial Chamber == The burial chamber is a two-tiered yokoana-style chamber, likely constructed of hewn stone. Measurements (based on Edo period and Meiji records): * Burial chamber: Approx. 5.5m in length, 3.6m in width, 3m in height. * Entrance corridor: Approx. 7.6m in length, 1.8m in width, 2.4m in height. It is thought that the chamber once contained a large house-typed stone coffin, fragments of which might have been removed during the Kamakura period. == Buried Person == The Imperial Household Agency attributes the tomb to {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}}, son of Emperor Yōmei and Anahobe no Hashihito. Historical records describe his death in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑紫国}} in 603, with burial later conducted at Hanyū Kōjō. == See Also == * {{ill|Prince Kume|ja|来目皇子}} * {{ill|Kuwasan Tōno-o Kofun|ja|桑山塔ノ尾古墳}} (Temporary burial site of Prince Kume) * {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} == External Links == *
